questions.prompt: What does it mean that an async function 'suspends' at an await, and why is that different from blocking?

options.text: Suspending pauses the function and frees its thread for other work, resuming later — so a handful of threads can service thousands of in-flight async operations, unlike a blocking call that holds the thread hostage until it completes.
Suspending and blocking are identical; await simply blocks the current thread until the result is ready.
Suspending spawns a new OS thread per await, which is why async code scales.
Suspending pauses the entire program, including all other tasks, until the awaited value arrives.

questions.explanation: The chapter's crucial distinction: awaiting suspends and frees the thread (few threads serve many operations), whereas blocking holds the thread. (b) erases the very distinction the chapter draws. (c) is false; the model avoids thread-per-operation. (d) is false; other tasks continue — only this function pauses.

questions.prompt: These two fetches are independent. What is wrong with this code from a latency standpoint, and how do you fix it?

options.text: Sequential awaits run sequentially — b doesn't start until a finishes — so two independent calls are needlessly serialized (total time is their sum). Fix by starting them concurrently with async let (or a task group) and awaiting afterward, making total time roughly the max, not the sum.
Nothing is wrong; separate await statements automatically run in parallel.
The bug is that await can't be used twice in one function; you must combine them into a single call.
The fix is to remove try, which is what forces sequential execution.

questions.explanation: The chapter's most common misread: sequential awaits serialize, so independent calls should use async let / a task group to overlap (time ≈ max, not sum). (b) is exactly the misconception the chapter corrects. (c) fabricates a one-await-per-function rule. (d) is false; try has nothing to do with sequencing.

questions.prompt: What does 'structured' mean in structured concurrency, and what problem does it prevent?

options.text: Child tasks have a lifetime bounded by a parent scope — a task group won't return until all its children have finished or been cancelled — which prevents orphaned background work from leaking past the function that started it.
It means tasks are stored in a structured data format like JSON for debugging.
It means every task must be declared at the top of the file in a fixed structure.
It means tasks run in a guaranteed sequential order determined by their declaration.

questions.explanation: The chapter: structured means child tasks are scope-bounded (the group won't return until children finish/cancel), preventing orphaned work. (b) and (c) fabricate unrelated meanings of 'structured.' (d) is false; children run concurrently, not in declaration order.

questions.prompt: How does an unstructured Task { } differ from a structured child task, and why does it matter?

options.text: An unstructured Task is not bound to a scope — it runs independently, and you're responsible for its lifetime and cancellation; it opts out of the safety structured concurrency provides, so it's the exception (bridging from non-async code, managed fire-and-forget), not the default.
An unstructured Task is automatically cancelled when its enclosing function returns, exactly like a structured child.
There is no difference; Task { } and task-group children behave identically.
Unstructured tasks cannot throw errors, while structured ones can.

questions.explanation: The chapter: an unstructured Task isn't scope-bound — you own its lifetime/cancellation — so it opts out of structured safety and is the exception, not the default. (b) is false; it is precisely NOT bound to the enclosing scope. (c) erases the key difference. (d) fabricates a throwing restriction.

questions.prompt: Cancellation in Swift concurrency is 'cooperative.' What does that mean in practice?

options.text: Cancelling a task only sets a flag; the task must check it (via Task.checkCancellation() or Task.isCancelled) and stop voluntarily. A tight compute loop that never checks will run to completion even after cancellation, so honoring cancellation is the programmer's responsibility.
Cancelling a task forcibly and immediately terminates it at whatever line it's executing, no cooperation needed.
Cancellation is impossible in structured concurrency; tasks always run to completion.
Cooperative means the OS scheduler decides whether to honor the cancellation, independent of the task's code.

questions.explanation: The chapter: cancellation sets a flag the task must check and honor; a loop that never checks runs to completion. (b) describes forcible cancellation, which Swift explicitly does NOT do. (c) is false; cancellation exists and propagates to children (as a marking). (d) misattributes the responsibility to the OS rather than the task's own checks.

questions.prompt: Per the chapter's trade-off, why isn't maximum concurrency (firing all child tasks at once) automatically the best choice?

options.text: Firing thousands of unbounded child tasks can exhaust connections or memory; task groups let you bound the width (e.g. process in batches), which is often the correct engineering choice over starting everything at once.
Maximum concurrency is always best; there is no downside to starting unlimited tasks simultaneously.
Concurrency should always be avoided; sequential code is universally safer and faster.
Task groups cannot limit the number of concurrent children, so width can't be bounded anyway.

questions.explanation: The chapter's trade-off: unbounded task fan-out can exhaust resources (connections, memory); bounding width via task groups (batching) is often better. (b) ignores resource limits. (c) overcorrects into avoiding concurrency entirely. (d) is false; bounding width via task groups is exactly the recommended technique.
